coredata.dc:title: Rapeseed oil residue-based fluorescent probes for bifunctional detection of Hg2+ and aureomycin hydrochloride
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiaxuan
Ma, Wensi
Huang, Xinyue
Jiang, Lei
Yuan, Lin
Ji, Xiuling
Tu, Yujiao
coredata.dc:description: Rapeseed oil residue (ROR) is the waste product of rapeseed oil extraction, mainly including protein and lignocellulose. In this study, we utilized waste ROR as the raw material to successfully prepare blue fluorescent carbon dots (ROR-CDs) by self-propagating synthesis. It demonstrated rapid preparation (20 min) with low energy consumption (400 °C) and without complicated purification processes. We analyzed the structural and optical properties of ROR-CDs. ROR-CDs exhibited exceptional fluorescence performance and stabilized, responding specifically to divalent mercury ions (Hg2+) and aureomycin hydrochloride (CTC). Thus, we developed a high-sensitivity detection technique with a detection range and limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ of 0–250 μg/L and 191.69 ng/L, respectively. For CTC, the detection range and LOD were 0–50 μM and 12.7 nM (S/N = 3), respectively. It is easy to operate and low-cost, ensuring efficient assistance for testing efforts in associated domains. Additionally, ROR-CDs-based detection technique can analyze Hg2+ and CTC in the actual samples, demonstrating significant potential for application in environmental monitoring and food safety assessment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-propagating synthesis
Fluorescent probe
Hg2+
Aureomycin hydrochloride
Limit of detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurements
Mechanisms on quenching and enhancement of ROR-CDs fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (inset: HR- TEM) (a), FTIR spectrum (b), and XRD pattern (c) of ROR-CDs. XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared ROR-CDs (d). High-resolution C1s spectra (e), N1s spectra (f), and O1s spectra (g).

UV–Vis and excitation-emission spectra of ROR-CDs (a). Fluorescence emission spectra and full-wavelength spectra of ROR-CDs under different excitations (b). The fluorescence of metal ions (c), vitamin (c), standard solutions (c), and antibiotics (d) to the ROR-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of ROR-CDs with various Hg2+ (0–250 μg/L) (a) and CTC (0–50 μM) concentrations (d). Linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency of ROR-CDs and Hg2+ (0–20 μg/L, 20–250 μg/L) concentrations (b, c). Linear relationship between fluorescence enhance efficiency of ROR-CDs and CTC (0–5 μM, 5–50 μM) concentrations (e, f).

UV absorption spectra of ROR-CDs, Hg2+, and ROR-CDs + Hg2+ (a). UV–vis absorption of Hg2+ and Ex, Em of RO-CDs (b). Decay lifetime of ROR-CDs, ROR-CDs + Hg2+ (c). Stern-Volmer equation for fluorescence quenching of ROR-CDs by Hg2+ at different temperatures (d).

The UV absorption spectra of ROR-CDs, CTC, and ROR-CDs + CTC (a). The fluorescence intensity of ROR-CDs with different concentrations of CTC at 436 nm (b). The zeta potential of ROR-CDs and ROR-CDs + CTC (c).
Comparison fluorescent detection performance of Hg2+.
Comparison fluorescent detection performance of CTC.
Analytical results of Hg2+ detection in the actual samples.
Analytical results of CTC detection in the actual samples.

head.ce:title: Rapeseed oil residue-based fluorescent probes for bifunctional detection of Hg2+ and aureomycin hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Kunming University, Kunming 650214, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Kunming University, Kunming 650214, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Food Inspection Centre, Kunming Institute for Food and Drug Control, Kunming 650032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kunming Institute for Food and Drug Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Food Inspection Centre, Kunming Institute for Food and Drug Control, Kunming 650032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical School, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical School, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and purification of green fluorescent ROR-CDs and its application in detecting Hg2+ and aureomycin hydrochloride.

Rapeseed oil residue (ROR) is the waste product of rapeseed oil extraction, mainly including protein and lignocellulose. In this study, we utilized waste ROR as the raw material to successfully prepare blue fluorescent carbon dots (ROR-CDs) by self-propagating synthesis. It demonstrated rapid preparation (20 min) with low energy consumption (400 °C) and without complicated purification processes. We analyzed the structural and optical properties of ROR-CDs. ROR-CDs exhibited exceptional fluorescence performance and stabilized, responding specifically to divalent mercury ions (Hg2+) and aureomycin hydrochloride (CTC). Thus, we developed a high-sensitivity detection technique with a detection range and limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ of 0–250 μg/L and 191.69 ng/L, respectively. For CTC, the detection range and LOD were 0–50 μM and 12.7 nM (S/N = 3), respectively. It is easy to operate and low-cost, ensuring efficient assistance for testing efforts in associated domains. Additionally, ROR-CDs-based detection technique can analyze Hg2+ and CTC in the actual samples, demonstrating significant potential for application in environmental monitoring and food safety assessment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg is a heavy metal pollutant that enters aquatic ecosystems through volcanic activity, mineral weathering, and various anthropogenic industrial processes, significantly affecting environmental health [1]. The Hg2+ is a notable contributor to heavy metal pollution in water. It can become highly concentrated within ecosystems [2], making it bioavailable and easily absorbed by the human body via the food chain. Exposure to and accumulation of Hg2+ can lead to serious health issues, including skin and eye irritation, as well as kidney toxicity [3].

Due to its effective antimicrobial properties, aureomycin hydrochloride (CTC) has found extensive application in animal husbandry [4]. However, various antibiotic residues, including CTC, are released into the environment through animal waste, which can negatively impact microbial communities and pose potential risks to ecosystems and human health throughout the food chain [5,6]. Consequently, developing rapid and straightforward detection methods for Hg2+ and CTC is crucial for safeguarding environmental integrity and protecting human health.

Various techniques have been employed to detect Hg2+, including atomic absorption spectroscopy [7], atomic fluorescence spectroscopy [8], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [9], and electrochemical analysis [10]. Additionally, most CTC detection mainly uses terahertz time-domain spectroscopy [11], surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy [12], ultra-efficient liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS) [13], and electrochemical analysis [14]. However, they are costly, depending on precious instruments, and can exhibit inadequate accuracy and stability in on-site detection. Fluorescence analysis techniques utilizing various fluorescent nanomaterials present a solution to these technical challenges [15,16]. Wang et al. developed a fluorescent probe Coa-SH based on coumarin for detecting Hg2+. Coa-SH exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg2+, making it more convenient and efficient compared to traditional detection methods [17]. Liu et al. constructed a novel ratio fluorescence sensor, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, for the specific recognition and sensitive detection of CTCs. The detection method based on SiC@ZIF-8@MIP boasts advantages such as fast response, wide linear range, and low limit of detection (LOD) [18]. Therefore, detection methods based on fluorescent materials represent a promising strategy for on-site food safety and public health security evaluation. Carbon dots (CDs) are particularly promising due to their abundant functional groups, improved biocompatibility, and lower toxicity compared to other nanomaterials, making them widely used in fluorescence detection [19–21]. Thus, developing a cost-effective, straightforward, precise, and stable detection method for quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and CTC using CDs holds significant potential.

Self-propagating synthesis is a thermochemical treatment process characterized by simplicity, low energy consumption, and rapid reaction conditions. In this process, the mixed reactants, once ignited by an external energy source, generate heat that propagates through the mixture of a combustion wave. It allows for the gradual formation of the target product without additional energy, thus saving time and resources. Self-propagating synthesis shows promising applications in carbon nanomaterials [22–24]. The product quality is excellent, high temperatures and rapid cooling facilitate the formation of regular structures, enhancing the optical properties and stability of CDs. The raw materials have strong adaptability, allowing for efficient utilization of waste carbon sources and reducing impurity effects. It is environmentally friendly, generating minimal waste or converting to by-products. The process is simple and easy to operate, making it suitable for industrial-scale production. Currently, utilizing biomass waste as a carbon source precursor for the environmentally friendly production of CDs has become increasingly popular [25,26]. It reduces the energy and equipment requirements during the carbonization of biomass materials, significantly lowering the preparation costs [27].

Rapeseed oil residue (ROR) is the waste product of rapeseed oil extraction, mainly including protein and lignocellulose [28]. With the development of modern industrial technology, oil residue as the precursor of various carbon materials has been widely used [29]. In this study, ROR-CDs were synthesized through a self-propagating reaction facilitated by nitrate and citric acid. The oil residue contributes various functional groups to the CDs during pyrolysis, enhancing the interaction with Hg2+ and CTC. Unlike traditional preparation methods, it eliminates the need for purification steps, such as centrifugation or dialysis. During the ROR pyrolysis process, most impurities evaporate from the system or are adsorbed by the reaction intermediates, resulting in a pure and stable product with excellent performance. Preliminary studies demonstrated that ROR-CDs exhibit a significant fluorescence quenching effect on Hg2+ while providing notable fluorescence enhancement for CTC. It presents a novel solution for detecting Hg2+ and CTC pollution, holding promising implications for advancements in materials science and environmental science.

The drug used in the experiment was citric acid (CA, C6H8O7), KNO3, NaCl, H2O2, Na2HPO4, CH3OH, HgCl2, As2O5, PbCl2, FeCl3, K2Cr2O7, AgNO3, CuCl2, CdCl3, BaCl2, ZnCl2, KCl, Vitamin C (VC), vitamin B1 (VB1), gentamicin (GEN), vitamin E (VE), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), Fleroxacin (FO), cefazolin (CEZ), cephalexin Monohydrate (CLX), cefquinome Sulfate (CES), enoxacin (ENC), cephalothin (CTX), spectinomycin (SH), kanamycin (KAN), erythromycin (EM), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), sparfloxacin (SPX), cefaclor (CEC), metronidazole (MTZ), cefradine (RAD), cefuroxim (CXM), ciprofloxacin (CIP), ceftazidime (CAZ), azithromycin (AZM), and chloramphenicol (CHL). These reagents were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The discarded rapeseed oil residue was taken from the local vegetable market. Ultrapure water was prepared by using the Milli-Q system. Standard solutions of chloride, nitrate, phosphate, and bicarbonate were provided by Xiamen Haibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Xiamen, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, the Netherlands) was used to obtain images of the CDs. The electron binding energy of surface elements was determined by X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA), and the surface chemical characteristics of CDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) measured the crystalline phase. The optical properties of the CDs were determined by the UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) and the G9800A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was determined by a fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, UK). Hg2+ in actual water samples was quantified by an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). CTC in chicken and milk samples was quantified by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Triple Quad 5500, AB SCIEX, USA).

The rapeseed oil residue was dried and grounded at 80 °C through the 60-drug sieve for use. The 10 g of rapeseed oil residue powder and 10 g of citric acid were mixed intoa beaker, added 50 mL of water and 50 mL of saturated potassium nitrate (0.32 g/mL) solution, stirred well at 70 °C to dry, and grounded into fine powder. The powder was placed on an electric heating plate, the temperature was precisely controlled to 400 °C, and the reaction was maintained for 20 min, then cooled at room temperature. An appropriate amount of water was added to completely dissolve the products, filter, and remove the potassium ions in the solution with cation exchange resin. ROR-CDs were prepared by freeze-drying and characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, and XRD.

The preparation conditions for ROR-CDs were optimized, including the ratio of rapeseed oil residue, citric acid, and potassium nitrate (mKNO3:mCA:mROR = 1:1:1.6, 1:1:3.2, 2:1:1.6, 2:1:3.2, 1:2:3.2), reaction temperature (200–1000 °C) and reaction time (10–60 min). Assess using quantum yield (QY).

In this study, the fluorescence stability of ROR-CDs at different pH, various concentrations of sodium chloride (NaCl), different concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and natural light and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation times was investigated. The 10 μL of ROR-CDs solution with a 1 mg/mL concentration was mixed with different concentrations of sodium chloride (NaCl) solutions and H2O2 solutions to dilute to 1.0 mL. Measure the fluorescence intensity of the ROR-CDs solution under various NaCl and H2O2 conditions to investigate the salt tolerance and antioxidant properties of ROR-CDs. Add water to dilute 10 μL of ROR-CDs solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL to 1.0 mL, and measure the fluorescence intensity changes under natural light and UV light exposure for 1 h, as well as under different pH conditions, to investigate the light-induced bleaching resistance and acid-base resistance of ROR-CDs. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

The selective determination of ROR-CDs involves the analysis of multiple metal ions, antibiotics, anions, amino acids, and vitamins. 10 μL of ROR-CDs at 1 mg/mL was mixed with 30 μL of 1 mM solution and diluted to a total volume of 1.0 mL with phosphate buffer at pH 8. After 4 min, the fluorescence intensity was determined. We investigated the effect of 30 μM Hg2+ and CTC on the fluorescence intensity of ROR-CDs under different pH conditions. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurements
ce:section.ce:para: An Xe lamp served as an excitation light source, while a photomultiplier tube (PMT) functioned as a detector for measuring QY using an FS5 spectrometer. The 1 mL ROR-CDs solution was used with a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL for sample determination. The excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelengths were carefully set to specific values to establish the baseline curve. During this process, the slit widths for both the excitation and emission spectra were adjusted to ensure that the intensity of the emission signal peaked at its maximum. Then, the emission spectra of the samples were recorded under the same experimental conditions. Ultimately, an accurate measurement of the QY was achieved by comparing the established baseline curve with the emission curves of the samples. All experiments were conducted in triplicate.

The fluorescence lifetime of 1 mL ROR-CDs solutions with a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL was determined using the time-dependent single photon counting technique (TCSPC). All experiments were performed in triplicate. The mathematical model utilized for calculating the fluorescence lifetime is expressed as follows:
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
n = 1 , 2 , 3 … …
τ =
∑
A n
τ
n
2
A n
τ n

Y = B + A1 *exp(−i/τ1 ) + ….….. + An *exp(−i/τn ) (n = 1, 2, 3……).where Y represents the fluorescence intensity, B is the baseline intensity, An * is the amplitude of the n th exponential decay component, τn is the corresponding fluorescence lifetime, τ is the time variable, and n represents the serial number of the exponential term (n = 1,2,3….…..).

To assess the feasibility of ROR-CDs in the actual samples, we detected Hg2+ in tap water, river water, and lake water. The tap water was sourced from the laboratory, while the river and lake water samples were collected from the Baoxiang River and Fuxian Lake, respectively. Chicken and milk samples were obtained from local supermarkets. Before the analysis of Hg2+, the water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min, followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm micropore filter. To evaluate recovery rates, various concentrations of Hg2+ standard solutions were introduced into the ambient water samples. The chicken and milk samples underwent pre-processing (GB 31658.17–2021), after which different concentrations of CTC standard solutions were added to the resulting matrix. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

Blue ROR-CDs were successfully synthesized by self-propagating synthesis using the resultant solution without purification. The QY of the ROR-CDs was measured as mKNO3:mCA:mROR = 1:1:1.6, with a reaction time of 20 min and a temperature of 400 °C (Fig. S1). During the pyrolysis process, citric acid functions as a reducing agent and fuel, facilitating a continuous reaction. Potassium nitrate is a strong oxidant and fuel agent during self-propagating synthesis, engaging in a REDOX reaction with the rapeseed oil residue. This reaction releases significant amounts of heat energy, which promotes ignition and sustains the combustion process. In high-temperature conditions, potassium nitrate can decompose to release oxygen and other gases, supporting the combustion and accelerating the overall reaction.

The self-propagating process was performed using a heating plate, with a beaker inverted to cover the reactants placed on the electric heating surface. This arrangement created a relatively closed environment, fostering the reaction under low-oxygen conditions and contributing to a stable temperature throughout the self-propagating synthesis. During this process, the primary by-product produced was biochar. In the subsequent filtration, the insoluble biochar was effectively separated from the soluble CDs. Due to its excellent adsorption properties, biochar can efficiently absorb impurities, enabling the simultaneous removal of the contaminants and the biochar.

As a by-product of the oil industry, the resource utilization of ROR not only significantly reduces environmental pollution but also conforms to the concept of circular economy [26]. The method demonstrated rapid preparation (20 min) with low energy consumption (400 °C). The reaction intermediate effectively adsorbs impurities, allowing the final product to require only ion exchange resin to remove potassium ions, thereby eliminating the need for centrifugation, dialysis, and other steps, and presenting a promising option for large-scale production. Compared with traditional carbon sources (such as pure chemicals or pretreatment biomass), the raw material cost of ROR is almost zero. The synthesis process does not require complicated purification steps, and it significantly shortens the reaction time by 80 % and cuts energy consumption by 50 % [24].

The morphological characteristics and size distribution of fluorescent ROR-CDs were analyzed using TEM. The ROR-CDs display a consistently spherical particle morphology, with an average diameter of approximately 2.98 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.223 nm, as indicated by the high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1 a). To further investigate the chemical functional group composition on the surface of ROR-CDs, FTIR analysis was conducted. The absorption peak observed at 3442.85 cm−1 suggested the stretching vibrational characteristics of the O-H bond, which may indicate the presence of hydroxyl groups or their interaction with other functional groups. It implied that the surface of ROR-CDs was rich in hydroxyl groups. The absorption peak at 1059.71 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of the C-O bond, which may be associated with the ether bond or the hydroxyl group. The absorption peak at 1654.88 cm−1 indicated the universality of CC–H bonds, which implied that the molecular structure may contain unsaturated carbon–carbon double bonds and hydrogen atoms linked to them. The absorption peak at 2928.16 cm−1 originated from the C–H vibration, further confirming the presence of C–H bonds. The absorption peak at 1237.57 cm−1 was generated by the C-N expansion vibration, indicating that the material may contain nitrogen-containing functional groups. Through comparative analysis of the infrared spectroscopy of ROR-CDs and ROR (Fig. 1b), the difference and similarity of absorption peaks at different wavenumbers can be more clearly identified, thus providing clear evidence for the composition of the surface functional groups of ROR-CDs and providing a theoretical basis for the deep understanding of their chemical properties and their potential applications. In addition, the 2θ value of ROR-CDs determined by XRD was 30.90, which has a high peak intensity, indicating that the content of the crystal surface in the material was relatively high, and the widened peak type indicated the small crystal size of the CDs or certain crystal defects. It was consistent with the crystal morphology observed with TEM (Fig. 1c).

To explore the surface state and element composition of ROR-CDs, XPS analysis was performed. The XPS spectra revealed four key peaks at 168.88 eV, 284.99 eV, 407.08 eV, and 532.16 eV that correspond to characteristic absorption peaks of the S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s (Fig. 1d). Analysis of the C1s spectrum showed that the four peaks at 284.75 eV, 287.93 eV, 292.59 eV, and 295.36 eV correspond to CC, C–O–C, CN, and –COOH, respectively (Fig. 1e). It indicated that carbon binded nitrogen and oxygen in the ROR-CDs structure; the presence of CN and C–O–C bonds implied the doping of nitrogen and oxygen in the carbon skeleton, and the appearance of –COOH functional group gave some acidic and hydrophilic properties. In the N1s spectrum, the two peaks at 400.47 eV and 407.19 eV corresponded to the N-H bond and graphene N, respectively (Fig. 1f). The N-H bond indicated the possibility of amino groups, and the graphene N may impact the electrical and optical properties of ROR-CDs. In the O1s spectrum, two peaks at 531.80 eV and 532.80 eV were attributed to C–O/–OH and O–H, respectively (Fig. 1g), which again emphasized the abundance of hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl and ether bonds in the material. The results of the XPS analysis provided information on the elemental composition and bonding properties of ROR-CDs. Due to the abundant hydrophilic functional groups, such as hydroxyl and amino groups in the material, ROR-CDs showed good water solubility, which provided favorable conditions for their application in biomedicine, environmental science, and other fields.

To study the optical properties of ROR-CDs, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption characteristics were analyzed in detail. ROR-CDs showed a significant absorption peak at 237 nm, attributed to the π-π* electron transition caused by the C=C bond of the sp2 (Fig. 2 a). The optimal excitation wavelength of the ROR-CDs was 350 nm, and a distinct emission peak was at 450 nm (Fig. 2b). When the excitation wavelength changed in the range of 280 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity showed a trend of strengthening first and then weakening, accompanied by the redshift phenomenon, which fully revealed the significant wavelength dependence of ROR-CDs (Fig. 2b), caused by the band gap of ROR-CDs being affected by different surface states and the non-uniform size distribution. This feature provided solid theoretical support and an experimental basis for the potential value of ROR-CDs in optical applications [30].

In this study, the sample concentration used in the fluorescence performance experiment of ROR-CDs was 0.01 mg/mL, and the sample amount was 1 mL. The ROR-CDs showed high stability within the pH range of 8 to 10 (Fig. S2a). The fluorescence intensity of ROR-CDs fluctuated slightly in the range of 0 to 2.0 M NaCl concentrations (Fig. S2b), and the fluorescence of ROR-CDs remained relatively stable after the addition of 0 to 0.1 M H2O2, indicating excellent antioxidant properties (Fig. S2c). Furthermore, ROR-CDs showed significant fluorescence stability within 60 min under natural and UV light (Fig. S2d and Fig. S2e). In terms of environmental stability, ROR-CDs can maintain fluorescence stability at the alkalinous pH (8–10), high salt (2 M NaCl), and oxidation conditions (0.1 M H2O2), which was better than the literature NCQDs (fluorescence decay at pH > 10) [31].

In the literature, Wang et al prepared Zn-loaded biochar using self-propagating synthesis [27]. However, the quantum yield of CDs (QY = 8.2 %) was lower than the QY achieved in this study (10.14 %). Although the QY of ROR-CDs was lower than that of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCQDs, QY = 15 %), its raw material cost and process simplification significantly improved the cost performance [31].

The selective recognition properties of ROR-CDs were explored. The results revealed that Hg2+ had a significant quenching effect on the blue fluorescence of ROR-CDs (Fig. 2c). CTC was the only compound that could enhance the fluorescence of ROR-CDs (Fig. 2d). Thus, ROR-CDs can be used as nanoprobes to detect Hg2+ and CTC.
ROR is rich in protein and lignocellulose, which can introduce functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 during high temperature, and enhance the interaction between CDs and Hg2+/CTC [25].

In the range of pH values between 2.2 and 10.0/12.0, we examined the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of ROR-CDs by adding Hg2+ and CTC. The results showed that adding Hg2+ could achieve the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency at pH 8.0 while adding CTC reagent could achieve the maximum fluorescence enhancement efficiency at pH 10.0 (Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b). The optimal reaction times were determined to be 4 min and 5 min, respectively (Fig. S4c and Fig. S4d).

The literature comparison showed that although Jaswal et al used nitrogen-sulfur co-doped CDs to detect Hg2+ (LOD = 35 nM) [26], its raw material needed complicated pretreatment. However, in this study, the ROR-CDs did not need pretreatment, the detection limit of Hg2+ was lower (191.69 ng/L), and the advantages of raw material and performance were significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms on quenching and enhancement of ROR-CDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para:
The quenching mechanisms of quenchers to fluorescence CDs mainly include photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching (SQ), and dynamic quenching (DQ). To investigate the mechanism of quenching ROR-CDs fluorescence by Hg2+, we analyzed the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4 a), and upon the addition of Hg2+, the absorption peak of ROR-CDs was red-shifted from 225 nm to 235 nm, indicating an interaction between Hg2+ and ROR-CDs. The excitation and emission bands of the fluorescence spectra of ROR-CDs did not overlap with the UV absorption peak of Hg2+ (Fig. 4b). After fitting lifetime values with a double exponential function, the fluorescence decay lifetime of ROR-CDs with different Hg2+ concentrations was essentially unchanged (Fig. 4c), combined with UV, indicating that ROR-CQDs and Hg2+ may form non-fluorescent ground state complexes, leading to fluorescence quenching of by N-CQDs [40]. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Hg2+ may be the SQ. The Stern-Volmer equation was used for further validation.
F
0
/F = 1 + KSV [c]=1 + Kq τ0 [c]

KSV is the dynamic quenching constant, Kq is the dynamic quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the average lifetime of the fluorophore (Fig. 4d). From the Stern-Volmer curves of Hg2+ at different temperatures, the slope (0.4106, 0.3326, 0.3150) and Kq [11.08 × 109 (g/L)−1 S−1, 7.95 × 109 (g/L)−1 S−1, 7.09 × 109 (g/L)−1 S−1] decreased with increasing temperature (288 K, 298 K, 310 K) (Fig. S7), so SQ played a role. Based on the effect of τ/τ0 = 1, the temperature on Kq, and UV–visible absorption, the quenching process of ROR-CDs fluorescence was SQ. More reports of SQ dominated by Hg2+ quenching of ROR-CDs fluorescence [41–43].

We examined the UV absorption spectra of ROR-CDs, CTC, and their combination (ROR-CDs + CTC) as shown in Fig. 5 a. Upon mixing CTC with the ROR-CDs solution, no new compounds were formed. The observed UV absorption peak for CTC at 388 nm exhibited a slight redshift and broadening, suggesting a binding interaction between CTC and ROR-CDs. The presence of electron-donating groups (–NH2, –OH) and electron-withdrawing substituents was confirmed through FT-IR and XPS analyses, indicating both π electron acceptor and π electron donor characteristics. It suggested that the NH2 and –OH groups of CTC may interact with the surface of the carbon dots via hydrogen bonding. Furthermore, the electron-rich groups of CTC (–NH2, –OH) and the electron-deficient groups (−Cl) can enhance the interaction between CTC and ROR-CDs through π-π electron donor–acceptor interactions, which contributed to the fluorescence enhancement of ROR-CDs. The attachment of CTC can modify the surface defects of the carbon dots, leading to an increase in fluorescence intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the binding constant (K) for CTC and ROR-CDs can be calculated using the Benesi-Hildebrand equation.
1
F -
F 0
=
1
K
F max
-
F 0
C
+
1
F max
-
F 0

F0 , F, and Fmax are the fluorescence intensities at 436 nm in the presence of CTC, at a 10 μM CTC concentration, and the saturated CTC concentration, respectively. C is the concentration of the CTC. The binding constant (K) was 7.87 × 104 M−1. The change in the UV–vis absorption spectrum and the binding constant of ROR-CDs suggested an interaction between ROR-CDs and CTC, leading to enhanced fluorescence. The results demonstrated that CTC induced the aggregation of ROR-CDs and enhanced the fluorescence of ROR-CDs. The aggregation of ROR-CDs can limit intramolecular vibrational motion and the rotation of surface functional groups of ROR-CDs to enhance π conjugation, resulting in increased radiative transition rate and decreased non-radiative transition rate, i.e., the increased fluorescence emission was due to the inhibition of non-radiative decay by the aggregation state. Thus, the fluorescence enhancement of ROR-CDs was due to the AIE [44–46].

To study the sensing mechanism, two aspects are considered. One is the fluorescence mechanism, which has been investigated in this work (AIE). The other is where the CDs interacted with the analytes. FTIR and XPS showed abundant amino groups on ROR-CDs, which further explained that ROR-CDs may aggregate due to the interaction between the amino groups on the surface of ROR-CDs and CTCs, which enhanced the fluorescence of ROR-CDs. We also investigated the mechanism of action between ROR-CDs and CTC through zeta potential (Fig. 5c). After adding CTC, the zeta potential of ROR-CDs changed from −36.27 to −25.15, due to the interaction between the nitrogen-rich structure (amino groups) on the surface of ROR-CDs and the carboxyl groups on CTC.

The AIE effect induced aggregation through hydrogen bonding and π-π with CTC and inhibited non-radiative transition. The SQ mechanism forms the ground-state complex through the coordination of Hg2+ and ROR-CDs surface –OH/–COOH, and the dynamic quenching constant (Kq) decreases with the temperature increase, indicating that the AIE mechanism induces ROR-CDs aggregation through CTC and enhances the radiation transition efficiency. In contrast, Deng et al detected CTC based on Ti3C2 QDs (LOD = 0.53 μM) with insufficient sensitivity [39]. Ren et al detected Hg2+ (LOD = 35 nM) based on SQ but did not achieve fluorescence enhancement [21]. The combined application of SQ and AIE in this study has unique advantages in dual-target detection and can effectively reduce cross-interference.

In the actual samples (tap water, river water, milk), the recovery of Hg2+/CTC was 94.78 % and 105.64 % (RSD < 5 %), respectively, meeting the international standard (GB 5009.268–2016), and highly consistented with ICP-MS/LC-MS results (error < 3 %). Compared with the literature, although the nitrogen-doped CDs can detect Hg2+ (LOD = 0.2 nM) in food [43], it did not cover CTC. The CDs developed by Zhou can detect Cr(VI)/Hg2+ (LOD = 0.1 μM) [41], but the recovery rate in the actual water samples fluctuated (RSD > 10 %). The ratio fluorescent test paper of He et al had high sensitivity (Hg2+ LOD = 0.5 nM) [43], but the preparation process was complex and not easy to popularize. The ratio fluorescence sensor needed dual emission CDs, and the cost increased by 30 % [44]. The universality and accuracy of this study were significantly better than the single-target detection method.

In the fluorescent probes field, it was the first time detecting Hg2+ and CTC with CDs prepared by self-propagating synthesis based on waste rapeseed oil residue. The detection range coverd the typical concentration of environmental and food samples (Hg2+ 0–250 μg/L; CTC 0–50 μM) and was significantly more sensitive than similar probes (Hg2+ LOD = 191.69 ng/L; CTC LOD = 12.7 nM). In this study, ROR-CDs fluorescent probes were successfully prepared, with low cost, high sensitivity, and good selectivity in Hg2+ and CTC detection. This study achieved breakthroughs in environmental protection, cost-effectiveness, detection performance through waste raw materials, the self-propagating synthesis process, dual-target detection, and the SQ/AIE coordination mechanism. In the future, we would improve QY through sulfur/metal doping, develop multi-target detection systems combined with machine learning, and promote the large-scale application of self-propagating synthesis. It provides an efficient method for environmental and food safety monitoring and has significant academic value and industrial potential.

Jiaxuan Liu, Wensi Ma, the conception and design of the study, acquisition of data and drafting the article. Xinyue Huang, Lin Yuan, and Lei Jinag, acquisition of data. Xiuling Ji, and Yujiao Tu, analysis and interpretation of data. All authors are approval of the version to be submitted.

Jiaxuan Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Wensi Ma: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Xinyue Huang: Validation, Investigation. Lei Jiang: Validation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Lin Yuan: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Xiuling Ji: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Yujiao Tu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

Yunnan Key Laboratory of Metal-Organic Molecular Materials and Device, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Kunming University (YNMO-YB-2405). Special Basic Cooperative Research Programs of Yunnan Provincial Undergraduate Universities' Association (202301BA070001-020, 202301BA070001-018). Science Research Foundation of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education of China (2023J0839, 2024Y755, 2023Y0904). Frontier Research Team of Kunming University 2023. Yunnan International Joint Laboratory of Research and Development of Crop Safety Production on Heavy Metal Pollution Areas (202403AP140035).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114165.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
